claim: The LLM Chess Leaderboard evaluates chess skill and instruction following in an agentic setting
LLM CHESS benchmark accepted at ICLR 2026, evaluating over 50 open and closed source models
Reasoning models have average win rate of 45.4% vs random opponent; non-reasoning models average 0.7%
o3 (low) achieves approximately 758 Elo against calibrated chess engine
DeepSeek-R1 performs worse than GPT-2 at chess, with high illegal move rate (>10%)
GPT-3.5-turbo-instruct plays at ~1750 Elo in chess, far outperforming chat-tuned models
Open-source models are universally terrible at chess due to insufficient chess data in pre-training
GPT-3.5-turbo-instruct estimated at 1750 Elo (+/- 50) against Stockfish, but makes illegal moves in 16% of games
LLMs fail at chess primarily due to impoverished domain knowledge in pre-training, not reasoning inability
ChessLLM fine-tuned model achieves 1788 Elo against Stockfish, winning 61% at skill level 0
GPT-3.5 Turbo Instruct initially most performant model in Continuation mode on dubesor leaderboard
LLM Chess Arena ranks Kimi K2.5 as top open-source model on leaderboard
Prompt engineering alone cannot produce more than 80% legal moves for DeepSeek-R1 in chess
In 2024, early models like GPT-4 and Claude 3.5 achieved 0% wins against random player
ChessArena finds thinking models like O3, Gemini-2.5-Pro perform best; prohibiting chain-of-thought severely impairs chess reasoning
RL training induces substantial positive shift in move quality distribution and reduces hallucination rates in chess
C1-4B model achieves 48.1% accuracy on chess puzzles, outperforming all open-source models and most proprietary ones
LLM chess performance degrades significantly on out-of-distribution (OOD) positions, suggesting reliance on memorization
Mixture-of-Agents combining strong-reasoning + strong-instruction-following models can double win rates
Adding legal-move constraint raises Llama-3 legal move rate from ~43% to 97.4%
LLM Chess Elo correlates positively with hard coding evaluations, showing relationship between chess and general reasoning
Maxim Saplin created the LLM Chess project and evaluated 13 chat models in 2024
LLM CHESS reduces overfitting and memorization while preventing benchmark saturation through dynamic stochastic interaction
50M parameter Chess-GPT learns linear emergent world representation of board state, tracking all pieces with 99.2% accuracy
QwQ-32B competes effectively with DeepSeek-R1 and o1-mini in reasoning tasks
PGN2FEN benchmark shows reasoning models maintain >90% FEN accuracy across all move lengths while non-reasoning models collapse beyond 20 moves
DeepSeek R1 ranks ~1050 Elo on LLM Chess Arena
DeepSeek Reasoner achieves only 6% full correctness on 81-100 move PGN2FEN task
Chat format/instruction tuning robustly cripples LLM chess performance compared to completion models
ChessQA finds reasoning improves accuracy by +14.7 percentage points on average across models
Tiny Chess Llama (2.3M params) achieves 1350-1400 Elo with 99.1% legal move rate
FEN format outperforms Unicode boards by up to +21.7 percentage points for some models
Providing move history reduces blunders dramatically from 11.2% to 1.6% for o4-mini
GPT-4.5-preview solves 84.8% of Lichess chess puzzles; mixtral-8x7b solves only 0.9%
Chess LLM Benchmark uses Glicko-2 rating system calibrated to Lichess Classical ratings
Ollama defaults to 4-bit quantization; Q4_K_M offers best balance of size and quality
Self-hosting Ollama needs 8GB RAM minimum, 16GB+ recommended for larger models
Q4_K_M quantization reduces VRAM ~4x vs FP16 while maintaining ~99% quality
Choose llama.cpp for <4 GPU deployments, vLLM for production serving with >8 GPUs
GLM-5 achieves best-in-class performance among open-source models on reasoning, coding, agentic tasks
Kimi K2.5 uses MoE architecture with 1T total parameters, 32B active per token
Qwen3 supports seamless switching between thinking and non-thinking modes
Llama 4 Maverick has 17B active parameters, 128 experts, 400B total parameters using MoE
GLM-5.1 has 754B total parameters, 40B active, 202K context window, released under MIT license
LLM Chess uses Komodo Dragon engine levels 1-5 (250-750 Elo) as anchors for Elo estimation
Non-reasoning models fail at instruction-following, not chess per se; reasoning models saturate random-based evaluations by 2025
Long round data supervision in ChessLLM outperforms short-round data by 350 Elo points
RL cannot overcome impoverished domain knowledge; chess receives minimal coverage in pretraining
Fine-tuning to predict best move leads to strong performance but unfaithful reasoning through RL
Self-consistency (54%) substantially outperforms multi-agent debate (46%) for LLM chess puzzle solving
